summary: Martha Nussbaum advocates for animal rights through the lens of the 'capabilities approach,' arguing that animals matter for their own sake and should be legally entitled to flourish according to their species-specific goals, rather than merely being protected from suffering.

principles.statement: Animals matter directly for their own sake, not because of their proximity or similarity to humans.
principles.rationale: Viewing animals with wonder reveals their intrinsic value, countering the anthropocentric view that they only matter if they possess human-like traits.
principles.source_quote: Wonder suggests to us that animals matter directly, for their own sake—not because of some similarity they have to ourselves.

principles.statement: We must focus on helping animals live the lives they want to live, striving for their own flourishing.
principles.rationale: Like humans, nonhuman animals are active beings trying to achieve their needs in a world that frequently blocks them.
principles.source_quote: We are all animals, thrown into this world together, striving to get the things we need, and often thwarted in the attempt.

principles.statement: Killing young animals is generally harder to justify than killing older ones.
principles.rationale: Animals are actively pursuing ends and generally seek to reach maturity as a primary life objective.
principles.source_quote: seek maturity as a central goal

principles.statement: Animals under human control should be guaranteed the opportunity to reproduce.
principles.rationale: Experiencing companionship and reproduction is a fundamental part of an animal's ability to flourish. Spaying or neutering is only permissible after they have had this chance.
principles.source_quote: at least one or two chances at sex and reproduction.

frameworks.name: The Capabilities Approach (for Animals)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining the legal rights, protections, and entitlements owed to different species of nonhuman animals.
steps: Consult experts who have lived closely with and studied a specific type of animal over long periods of time.
Determine what 'flourishing' entails for that specific species and individual.
Draft a 'legally enforceable constitution' of entitlements tailored to that kind of animal (e.g., granting dolphins the right to roam and socialize).
frameworks.source_quote: experts who have lived closely with a certain type of animal and studied those animals over long periods of time

mental_models.name: Striving for Flourishing
mental_models.description: A lens that views animals not merely as passive vessels capable of suffering, but as active agents with their own goals, seeking maturity and striving to flourish in species-specific ways.
mental_models.example: Recognizing that 'companion animals' (pets) exist not merely to please their owners, but to flourish in their own ways, requiring us to guarantee them natural experiences like companionship.

signature_quotes.text: Wonder suggests to us that animals matter directly, for their own sake—not because of some similarity they have to ourselves.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why animals have intrinsic value independent of human traits.

signature_quotes.text: We are all animals, thrown into this world together, striving to get the things we need, and often thwarted in the attempt.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the shared condition of humans and nonhuman animals.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It puts humans at the center of the moral universe and only values animals to the extent that 'they are (almost) like us,' which misses what makes animal life meaningful to the animals themselves.

anti_patterns.description: Focusing exclusively on an animal's capacity to suffer.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: While preventing suffering is important, this pitiless view reduces creatures to a scale of sentience and ignores their active pursuit of goals and their desire to flourish.
